questions.question: What conservation projects or events are currently advertised?
questions.answer: The sources advertise community involvement in combatting the Spotted Lanternfly through participation in community clean-up events and volunteer programs aimed at removing lanternflies and their eggs. The Conservation Board also encourages public attendance at its monthly meetings (usually the first Monday of the month) at Town Hall for those interested in conservation efforts. Additional conservation resources are referenced at https://mynewcastle.org/conservation-board, https://www.healthyyards.org/, and https://www.pollinator-pathway.org/.
questions.gap: The sources mention community clean-up events and volunteer programs for Spotted Lanternfly removal but lack specific details on goals, locations, dates, who can participate, and frequency. No structured project listings aligned with best practices are present.
nextPrompts: Are there specific upcoming conservation events with dates and locations listed on the New Castle Conservation Board webpage or event calendar?
Does the Town of New Castle publish a schedule or registration process for Spotted Lanternfly volunteer clean-up events?

questions.question: What documentation or photo/videos showing past projects exists online?
questions.answer: Not specified in the provided sources.
questions.gap: The provided sources contain no documentation, photos, videos, or online portfolio of past projects. Best practice calls for a public project portfolio with dated photos/videos, before-and-after documentation, and brief narratives of completed conservation work.
nextPrompts: Does the Town of New Castle Conservation Board maintain a project gallery or archive on mynewcastleny.gov or social media?
Are there any annual reports or newsletters from the Conservation Board that document completed on-the-ground projects with photos?

questions.question: What range of opportunities exist, from shared space maintenance to home landscaping to surveillance and reporting, and how easy is it for the average person to donate time or resources to get involved?
questions.answer: The sources describe two specific opportunities for community involvement: (1) Home landscaping — residents are encouraged to plant landscape buffers along property lines, with guidance on plant selection and design to reduce CO2, attract pollinators, limit soil erosion, and aid water retention. The Town's Environmental Coordinator (914-238-4723) and resources like healthyyards.org and pollinator-pathway.org are referenced for support. (2) Surveillance and reporting — residents are asked to identify and report Spotted Lanternfly sightings to the NYS Department of Agriculture and Markets or Cornell, with DEC providing additional reporting guidelines. Attendance at Environmental Board meetings is also mentioned as a way to get involved. No information is provided about shared space maintenance programs, volunteer coordination, or how to donate time or financial resources.
questions.gap: Sources cover home landscaping guidance and pest reporting but lack information on shared space maintenance programs, volunteer coordination, or mechanisms for donating time or resources. A full spectrum of engagement opportunities with clear onboarding instructions is absent.
nextPrompts: Does New Castle have a volunteer or community stewardship program for parks or shared green spaces?
